ASBESTOS LAW AND MOTION 503, DEFENDANT KAISER GYPSUM COMPANY, INC.'S MOTION TO DISMISS OR STAY FOR FORUM NON CONVENIENS. ARGUED AND THE COURT ADOPTS ITS RULING AS FOLLOWS: DENIED. DEFENDANT FAILED TO DEMONSTRATE THAT VIRGINIA IS A SUITABLE ALTERNATIVE FORUM BECAUSE IT DID NOT SHOW THAT ALL DEFENDANTS ARE SUBJECT TO JURISDICTION THERE. (SEE STANGVIK V. SHILEY, INC. (1991) 54 CAL.3D 744; AMERICAN CEMWOOD CORP. V. AMERICAN HOME ASSURANCE CO. (2001) 87 CAL.APP.4TH 431.) DEFENDANT'S ALTERNATE MOTION TO STAY THE ACTION IS DENIED. DEFENDANT FAILED TO DEMONSTRATE THAT CALIFORNIA IS A SERIOUSLY INCONVENIENT FORUM AS THE BALANCE OF PRIVATE AND PUBLIC INTEREST FACTORS MAKES IT JUST THAT THE LITIGATION REMAIN IN CALIFORNIA. FURTHER, THE COURT INDICATES THIS RULING IS WITHOUT PREJUDICE. ORDER SIGNED. JUDGE: TERI L. JACKSON, CLERK: E. LARNAUTI, REPORTER: KYLE MCLEAN, CSR #13787 OF AIKEN WELCH

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, For Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Oct-15-2015 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Ana T. Portillo of HAWKINS PARNELL THACKSTON & YOUNG (415-766-3221) appearing for defendants / moving parties Lowe's Companies, Inc. and Lowe's Home Centers, LLC
Kimberly J. Chu of BRAYTON & PURCELL LLP (415-898-1555), appearing for plaintiffs / opposing parties Benjamin J. Morehead and Rosemarie Morehead

RULING:
DEFENDANTS LOWE'S COMPANIES, INC. AND LOWE'S HOME CENTERS, LLC'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT OR, IN THE ALTERNATIVE, FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION

Hearing held and tentative ruling adopted as clarified: Defendants Lowe's Companies, Inc. and Lowe's Home Centers, LLC's motion for summary judgment is denied and its alternate motion for summary adjudication of plaintiffs' second cause of action for products liability - survival is granted. As to summary judgment, pursuant to the three step governmental interest analysis, Virginia's Statute of Repose (VA Code Ann. 8.01-250) applies. Plaintiffs concede that Virginia law applies to this issue. However, Virginia's Statute of Repose does not bar plaintiffs' claims. At a minimum, there is a triable issue whether the joint compound at issue to which Mr. Morehead was exposed was incorporated into an improvement to real property. This pertains to both the exposures occurring from the sanding of joint compound that had previously been applied to the wall as well as the cleaning up of joint compound that had previously fallen to the floor without ever having been applied to a wall. The issues of joint compound and respose are jury issues. As to summary adjudication of plaintiffs' second cause of action for products liability, pursuant to the three-step governmental interest analysis, Virginia law applies. First, the laws of Virginia and California differ with respect to strict liability, a fact plaintiffs do not dispute. Second, in examining each jurisdiction's interest in the application of its own law under the circumstances of the particular case to determine whether a true conflict exists, there is not a true conflict as Virginia has an interest in the application of its law while California does not. The Court does not get to the third step of the analysis however, even if there were a true conflict, Virginia's interest would be more impaired if its policy were subordinated to the policy of California. Overall, defendants' alleged supply of asbestos-containing joint compound occurred in Virginia; decedent's exposure to this joint compound occurred in Virginia; the exposure occurred while decedent was a Virginia resident; and decedent was a Virginia resident at the time he was diagnosed with his asbestos-related illness(es). Thus plaintiffs' second cause of action is barred pursuant to Virginia law. Counsel for plaintiffs is to prepare a proposed order in compliance with CRC 3.1312. Judge: Teri L. Jackson, Reporter: Tara L. Williams of AIKEN WELCH, CSR #13428 (Tel: 510-451-1580) (503/TLJ)

Oren Noah of Brayton Purcell (415.898.1555) appearing for plaintiff and responding party; Annie C. Wu of DeHay Elliston LLP (510.285.0750) for defendant and moving party; Florence A. Peebles of Berry & Berry (510.250.0430), designated defense counsel.

Argued and the Court adopts its ruling as follows: denied. Defendant failed to demonstrate that Virginia is a suitable alternative forum because it did not show that all defendants are subject to jurisdiction there. (See Stangvik v. Shiley, Inc. (1991) 54 Cal.3d 744; American Cemwood Corp. v. American Home Assurance Co. (2001) 87 Cal.App.4th 431.) Defendant's alternate motion to stay the action is denied. Defendant failed to demonstrate that California is a seriously inconvenient forum as the balance of private and public interest factors makes it just that the litigation remain in California. Further, the Court indicates this ruling is without prejudice. Order signed. Judge: Teri L. Jackson, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Reporter: Kyle McLean, CSR #13787 of Aiken Welch (Tel: 510.451.1580) (503/TLJ)
